region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia — widely adopted in Singapore Malay nasi padang culture
food_subgroup: Fried tempeh and tofu in sambal-coconut sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sambal Goreng. Closest parent is a fried tempeh/tofu dish with sambal sauce. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried tempeh and tofu with sambal chilli-coconut sauce (Malay sambal goreng style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JEG used as closest parent for fermented soybean-based fried dish with sambal sauce; no exact code exists for this composite Malay preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Sambal Goreng as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side-dish serving of sambal goreng at a nasi padang stall or home meal is approximately 120 g, providing around 238 kcal, 15 g protein, and 576 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Goreng (tempeh, tofu, long beans) — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central component data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Sambal Goreng (tempeh, tofu, long beans) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Micronutrient values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for tempeh (USDA #16113), firm tofu (USDA #16126), and long beans, as these specific micronutrients are not individually reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish. All values are consistent with the verified HPB/IMR primary data.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Tempeh (fermented soy, GI ~15–20) and firm tofu (GI ~15); composite dish GI estimated at ~35 based on dominant low-GI protein-rich ingredients and fat content from coconut milk and frying
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sambal Goreng as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for tempeh (GI ~15–20) and firm tofu (GI ~15), which are the dominant protein-rich ingredients. The presence of dietary fibre from long beans (2.8 g/100g), fat from coconut milk and frying oil, and the low net carbohydrate content (8.5 g/100g) all attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving = (35 × 8.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 3.57, rounded to 3.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from belacan (shrimp paste) and added salt
hypertension.guidance: A 120 g side-dish serving delivers approximately 576 mg sodium (~25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), primarily from belacan; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g and request reduced-belacan preparations where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~35) and low glycaemic load per serving (~3.6) due to high plant protein and fibre from tempeh, tofu, and long beans
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal goreng is well-suited for blood glucose management; its very low GI (~35) and GL (~3.6 per 120 g serving), combined with 15 g protein and tempeh isoflavones (daidzein, genistein) that improve insulin sensitivity, make it a favourable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk and frying oil; partially offset by plant sterols and isoflavones from tempeh and tofu
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 5 g saturated fat from coconut milk and frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and frequency, while benefiting from tempeh's plant sterols and soy isoflavones which have LDL-lowering properties at regular dietary intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from tempeh fermentation (equol from daidzein) and sambal spice base
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tempeh fermentation produces equol and prebiotic fibres that support gut microbiome diversity and reduce systemic inflammation; the sambal spice base (dried chilli, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaf) provides additional anti-inflammatory phytochemicals, making this dish generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Goreng (Tempeh, Tofu and Long Beans in Sambal) is a Javanese-Malay side dish that is a cornerstone of nasi padang, nasi ambeng, and Hari Raya celebratory meals in Singapore and Indonesia. Firm tofu (tauhu) and tempeh (fermented soybean cake) are cubed and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then stir-fried together with blanched long beans (kacang panjang) in a fragrant sambal paste made from dried chillies, shallots, garlic, and shrimp paste (belacan), finished with a touch of coconut milk for creaminess and body. The result is a complex, savoury-spicy dish with contrasting textures — crispy tempeh, silky tofu, and tender long beans — coated in an aromatic, slightly sweet-heat sauce. Nutritionally, sambal goreng stands out among Malay side dishes for its high plant protein content (12.5 g/100g), very low glycaemic index (~35), and isoflavones (daidzein, genistein) from tempeh that support insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular health. Sodium is moderate-to-high at 480 mg/100g due to belacan, and saturated fat is present from coconut milk and frying oil (4.2 g/100g). A 120 g side-dish serving provides approximately 238 kcal, 15 g protein, 16 g fat, 10 g carbohydrate, and 576 mg sodium. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is widely available at Singapore hawker centres and nasi padang stalls.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (verified, confidence 1.0 for core macros and most micronutrients), cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for tempeh and tofu (confidence 0.9). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods (tempeh, tofu) with documented basis. Overall record confidence is high given the strong primary source coverage for this well-documented Singapore Malay dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite sambal goreng preparation are not individually reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo verified data). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — tempeh, tofu, and long beans contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central and HPB Foodo). Copper (0.2 mg/100g), manganese (0.4 mg/100g), and vitamin K (15.0 mcg/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for tempeh (USDA #16113) and firm tofu (USDA #16126) as these micronutrients are not individually reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish. GI of ~35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for tempeh and tofu; no direct GI measurement for sambal goreng as a composite dish has been published. FoodEx2 code A0JEG used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Malay composite preparation. Two default portions are noted in the source data (150g and 120g); 120g is used as the canonical side-dish serving for clinical calculations as it is the verified HPB-aligned portion.
